title: Advanced Authoring: Files, Scripts, and Evals
objective: Apply the craft that separates a real skill from a wordy one: bundled files one level deep, utility scripts, workflows and feedback loops, and evaluation-driven development.

sources.title: Skill authoring best practices (Claude Platform Docs)
Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills (Anthropic Engineering)
sources.trust_rationale: Anthropic's own engineering blog explaining the design.

beats.narration: When a SKILL.md starts getting unwieldy — the guidance is to keep the body under about five hundred lines — split it. Move form-filling into FORMS.md, the full API into reference.md, examples into their own file, and scripts into a scripts folder. The SKILL.md becomes a lean table of contents, and Claude reads each piece only when the task calls for it.

visual.alt: A skill directory with SKILL.md plus bundled reference files and a scripts folder

beats.narration: There's a subtle trap here: keep references one level deep from SKILL.md. If a file points to another file that points to a third, Claude may only partially read the chain — previewing with something like head minus one hundred — and miss content. So link every reference file directly from SKILL.md. And for any reference longer than a hundred lines, add a table of contents at the top so Claude sees the full scope even on a partial read.

visual.alt: A too-deep nested reference chain versus references all one level deep

beats.narration: Scripts deserve real care. A pre-written utility script is more reliable than code Claude generates on the fly, it's deterministic, and it saves tokens because the code never enters context — only its output does. Two rules: solve, don't punt — handle errors inside the script rather than failing for Claude to sort out — and make intent explicit, saying run this script to execute, or see this script for the algorithm to read it as reference.

visual.alt: Reasons to prewrite scripts and the solve-dont-punt principle

beats.narration: For anything multi-step, structure it as a workflow. Give Claude a checklist it can copy and tick off, so it doesn't skip a critical step like validation. Even better, build a feedback loop: run a validator, fix the errors it reports, repeat until it passes. The validator can be a script, or it can just be a STYLE_GUIDE that Claude checks its own draft against. Either way, quality goes up sharply.

visual.alt: A copyable checklist and a run-validator, fix, repeat feedback loop

beats.prompt: Why should bundled reference files link directly from SKILL.md rather than chaining through each other?

options.text: Claude may only partially read nested files and miss content
Deep nesting is forbidden by the file format itself
Nested files always load fully into context at startup

beats.correct_feedback: Correct. Nested references risk partial reads, so keep everything one level deep from SKILL.md.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. It's because Claude may only partially read nested files and miss content, so keep every reference one level deep from SKILL.md.

beats.narration: Finally, how you develop a skill matters as much as how you write it. Anthropic's advice is to build evaluations before extensive documentation: run Claude on real tasks with no skill, note where it fails, and write just enough to fix those gaps. A powerful pattern is two Claudes — Claude A helps you author and refine the skill, while a fresh Claude B tests it on real tasks. You watch Claude B, then bring the gaps back to Claude A.

visual.alt: The five-step evaluation-driven development loop with Claude A authoring and Claude B testing

beats.prompt: What does evaluation-driven development recommend doing first?

options.text: Find real gaps and build evals before writing extensive docs
Write the complete documentation, then test at the very end
Ship the skill immediately and gather feedback in production

beats.correct_feedback: Right. Identify gaps and create evaluations first, so the skill solves real problems.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Evaluation-driven development says to find real gaps and build evaluations first, before writing extensive documentation.
